issues.summary: Pre-existing pytest failure: test_backfill_slugs_retry_loop — table recipe missing status column
issues.description:
What's failing
{{tests/test_migration_backfill_slug.py::test_backfill_slugs_retry_loop}} fails on every CI run since SSR + data-model PR (adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegood.com#116, commit {{ade81bc}}):
{{}}{{sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: (sqlite3.OperationalError) table recipe has no column named status[SQL: INSERT INTO recipe (id, user_id, guest_session_id, name, status, slug, is_public, data, created_at, updated_at) VALUES (...), (...), (...)]}}{{}}
The Recipe model (models/recipe.py) clearly declares status = db.Column(db.String(20), nullable=False, default='ready'), so db.create_all() should create the column.
Repro
{{}}{{bashcd Backenduv run pytest tests/test_migration_backfill_slug.py::test_backfill_slugs_retry_loop -xvs}}{{}}
Why it's pre-existing
Test was added in adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegood.com#116 alongside the status/slug/is_public columns. CI has been failing on this single test since merge. v0.2.0 shipped with this failure already in place; v0.2.1 (adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894) admin-merged through it because it's not a regression.
Hypothesis for the fix
The app fixture in tests/test_migration_backfill_slug.py calls create_app() first and then updates SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI to sqlite:///:memory:. By the time db.create_all() runs, Flask-SQLAlchemy may already have bound an engine to whatever URI create_app() defaulted to, so the :memory: override doesn't actually take effect — meaning create_all() runs against an existing on-disk DB that was created before status was added.
{{}}{{python
Current (broken) fixture:
app = create_app()app.config.update({"TESTING": True,"SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI": "sqlite:///:memory:"})}}{{}}
Likely fix — pass the URI into create_app so the engine is initialized with the right URI from the start:
{{}}{{python@pytest.fixturedef app():app = create_app(SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI="sqlite:///:memory:",TESTING=True,)with app.app_context():db.create_all()yield appdb.session.remove()db.drop_all(

issues.summary: Solo-repo merge friction: ruleset requires review approval, auto-merge disabled
issues.description:
What happens today
Repo settings:
Branch ruleset on {{main}} requires {{required_approving_review_count: 1}}
{{allow_auto_merge: false}} at the repo level
Solo development — no other reviewers exist
Result: every PR to main needs {{gh pr merge --admin}} (admin bypass). v0.2.1 (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894) merged this way today. v0.2.0 (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2891) did the same.
Why this is friction
Every release ships with an admin override, which:
Bypasses CI gates if any are red (we did this for the pre-existing Backend pytest failure)
Bypasses the (theoretical) review gate (no reviewer exists)
Leaves no audit trail of approval — admin override is just a permission, not a signal
Cannot use {{gh pr merge --auto --admin}} to queue a merge for "when CI passes" — auto-merge is disabled at repo level
Manual interactive merge step every time
Options
A) Open up auto-merge.
{{gh repo edit --enable-auto-merge}}
Future flow: {{gh pr merge --auto --admin --squash}} queues the merge; GitHub merges as soon as required checks clear (or immediately with admin)
Still needs admin override for the review-required rule, but at least CI is honored
B) Drop the review-required rule for solo workflows.
Edit ruleset to remove {{pull_request.required_approving_review_count}}
Keep CI required-checks rule so green CI is enforced
{{gh pr merge --auto --squash}} works without admin
C) Add a "self-approve via separate identity" pattern.
Use a bot account / second machine identity to review and approve own PRs
Heavyweight; not worth it for solo
D) Leave as-is.
Admin override every time
Status quo
Recommendation
A or B (or both): A is no-cost and clears one papercut; B is the honest reflection of solo workflow.
Source
Deferred from v0.2.1 release work (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894). Surfaced when GitHub blocked auto-merge.

issues.summary: Decide on .cloudbuildignore (currently absent — gcloud only reads .gcloudignore)
issues.description:
Context
The original /ship request for v0.2.1 included: "the dirs that don't belong in the build image for cloud-run should be ignored by .dockerignore and .cloudbuildignore or .gcloudignore or all three."
What I added in v0.2.1 (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894):
{{.dockerignore}} — read by Docker during {{docker build}} (the actual image build). ✅
{{.gcloudignore}} — read by {{gcloud builds submit}} to decide what to upload as the build source. ✅
{{.cloudbuildignore}} — does NOT exist as a documented gcloud feature. {{gcloud}} only reads {{.gcloudignore}}. Cloud Build does not have a native {{.cloudbuildignore}}.
So adding a {{.cloudbuildignore}} would be an orphan file that no tool reads.
Question
Do you want one anyway? Reasons to add:
Symmetry / future-proofing if a custom builder ever reads it
Documentation: the file's existence + comment header tells readers what would be ignored if such a feature existed
Some non-Google CI tools that build via Cloud Build APIs may read it
Reasons to leave absent:
Confusing — readers may think it's load-bearing when it isn't
Drift risk — copies of {{.gcloudignore}} patterns will diverge over time
What to do
Either:
A) Add {{.cloudbuildignore}} as a symlink to {{.gcloudignore}} so they can never driftB) Add {{.cloudbuildignore}} as a copy with a comment explaining it's defensiveC) Leave absent (current state) — close this issue as wontfix
Source
Deferred from v0.2.1 release work (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894). User asked for all three; we shipped two with this note.

issues.summary: Verify Cloud Build tag-push trigger regex matches ^v[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$
issues.description:
Context
Documented in CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md (release flow section): the Cloud Build trigger that deploys to Cloud Run on tag push MUST use the regex {{^v[0-9].[0-9].[0-9]+$}} so pre-release tags ({{v0.3.0-rc.1}}, {{v0.3.0-beta}}) and metadata tags ({{v0.3.0+build.123}}) cannot accidentally trigger a production deploy.
What's unknown
{{v0.2.0}} and {{v0.2.1}} tag pushes both fired the trigger green (verified in GCP Console), so a trigger DOES exist and IS firing. But:
{{}}{{bashgcloud builds triggers list --project=comdottasteslikegood
returns: Listed 0 items.
gcloud beta builds triggers list --project=comdottasteslikegood
returns: Listed 0 items.
}}{{}}
The CLI can't see it. Possibilities:
It's a 2nd-gen trigger in a non-default region
It's configured at the org level (not project)
It's owned by a different gcloud config / service account
It's the new GitHub-connected source trigger that uses a different list endpoint
Risk if loose
If the actual Console regex is loose (e.g., {{v.}}, ^v\\d+, {{v[0-9].}}), a future tag like {{v0.3.0-rc.1}} or {{v0.2.1-fix}} would trigger a production Cloud Run deploy of unstable code.
What to do
Open GCP Console → Cloud Build → Triggers
Find the tag-push trigger for this repo
Confirm the tag pattern is exactly {{^v[0-9].[0-9].[0-9]+$}}
If looser, tighten it
Bonus: figure out why the trigger isn't visible to {{gcloud builds triggers list}} and document the right query in CLAUDE.md
Acceptance
Console regex matches CLAUDE.md docs
A test push of {{v0.0.0-test}} does NOT trigger a build
A test push of {{v0.0.0}} DOES trigger a build (then delete)
Source
Deferred from v0.2.1 release work (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894). Original session discussion noted CLI/Console mismatch.

issues.summary: Reconcile .gcloudignore .git exclusion with cloudbuild.yaml submodule init
issues.description:
The conflict
{{cloudbuild.yaml}} runs {{git submodule update --init --recursive}} to pull {{Backend/}}, {{alirez-claude-skills/}}, and {{gemstack/}}. That command needs {{.git}} and {{.gitmodules}} to be present in the build's source tree.
{{.gcloudignore}} (line 1) excludes {{.git}}. So if anyone runs {{gcloud builds submit --config=cloudbuild.yaml}} from a local dir, the source archive uploaded to Cloud Build would not contain {{.git}}, and the submodule-init step would fail with {{fatal: not a git repository}}.
Why this presumably works today
The Cloud Build trigger that fires on tag push (the one we use for production deploys) is GitHub-connected — Cloud Build clones the repo directly from GitHub, including {{.git}}. {{.gcloudignore}} is irrelevant for trigger-based builds; it only matters for {{gcloud builds submit}} from a local dir.
But:
This is fragile. Anyone who reads CLAUDE.md and runs the documented {{gcloud builds submit --config=cloudbuild.yaml}} will hit a confusing failure.
We have no automated verification that the trigger uses GitHub source vs. gcloud-uploaded source.
What to do
Either:
A) Update CLAUDE.md to remove the {{gcloud builds submit}} instructions, since they don't actually work, and replace with the trigger-based deploy path (push tag → trigger fires).
B) Make {{gcloud builds submit}} actually work by adjusting {{.gcloudignore}} (remove {{.git}}) so a local source upload includes the git history. Verify the submodule init step works.
C) Keep both options but add a clear note in CLAUDE.md: "If you're running {{gcloud builds submit}} locally, comment out {{.git}} in {{.gcloudignore}} first."
Source
Deferred from v0.2.1 release work (PR adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2894). Spotted while expanding {{.gcloudignore}} for build-image trimming.

issues.summary: Bump protobufjs to >=7.6.3 — high-severity advisories blocked by Dependency Review license allow-list
issues.description:
Problem
protobufjs@7.5.5 (transitive, via @google-cloud/secret-manager → google-gax → @grpc/proto-loader) carries two high-severity advisories:
Code injection through bytes field defaults in generated toObject code
Denial of service from crafted field names in generated code
Both are fixed in protobufjs@7.6.3+.
Why it isn't just a bump
Versions ≥7.6.3 publish the license as BSD-3-Clause AND LicenseRef-scancode-protobuf. The Dependency Review workflow's allow-licenses list (MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD-2/3-Clause, ISC, …) rejects the compound expression, so any PR bumping the package fails the Dependency Review check on licensing — this was hit directly during adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2997 (a full lockfile regen pulled in 7.6.4 and the check failed with "incompatible licenses").
The LicenseRef-scancode-protobuf component is Google's protobuf license (BSD-style with a sublicensing clause) — the actual license didn't change; upstream just made the SPDX metadata more precise.
Options
Allow-list exception (recommended): add allow-dependencies-licenses for pkg:npm/protobufjs in the dependency-review workflow config, then bump the package (a targeted npm update protobufjs — see adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2997's surgical-lockfile approach to avoid churning unrelated transitives).
Add LicenseRef-scancode-protobuf handling if the action supports raw license-ref allowance.
Wait for upstream deps (@grpc/proto-loader etc.) to require ≥7.6.3, which forces the same license decision anyway.
Acceptance
[ ] npm audit --audit-level=high reports 0 high findings for protobufjs
[ ] Dependency Review passes on the bump PR
[ ] No unrelated lockfile churn
Context: found during the Angular 22 upgrade line of work (adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2996 → adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#2997 → #2999 → adamtasteslikegood/tasteslikegoodtheangularsvegancookbook#3009).

⚠️ Maintainability
The from google.api import metric_pb2 import is placed inside the function body rather than at the module top-level, which re-executes the import on every call to list_available_metrics.
+++ Details
📖 Explanation: Moving the import to the top of the file follows Python conventions and avoids the (minor) overhead of repeated import lookups on each invocation of the function.
+++
Uses AI. Verify results. Give Feedback

issues.summary: Express layer has no input validation — CLAUDE.md documents a server/validation.ts that doesn't exist; express-validator installed but never imported
issues.description:
Problem
Surfaced while reverse-engineering the stack for the PRD (PR #3082, prd/README.md § Known gaps).
CLAUDE.md's Layer 2 description lists:
server/validation.ts — express-validator rules for AI endpoints
That file does not exist — server/ contains only index.ts, proxy.ts, security.ts, valkey.ts, types.ts and tests. express-validator@^7.3.2 is declared in package.json (line 46) but is imported nowhere in the codebase, so it ships as dead weight.
Impact
There is zero Express-layer input validation. All /api/* traffic is streamed raw to Flask, and Flask's 10–500-character prompt check is the only gate on the AI endpoints. Rate limiting (server/security.ts) is the sole Express-side defense.
Options
Implement it — add the documented server/validation.ts with express-validator rules for the AI endpoints (/api/generate, /api/generate_image) and mount before the proxy. Note the proxy is intentionally mounted before express.json(), so validation middleware must not consume the body stream Flask expects — either validate query/headers only, or buffer-and-replay.
Descope it — remove express-validator from package.json, correct CLAUDE.md to say validation lives in Flask, and rely on Flask-side validation (Backend/validators/).
Either way, CLAUDE.md must stop describing a file that doesn't exist.
Filed by Claude on Adam's behalf

issues.summary: extensions.cache is a no-op stub — no response caching actually happens
issues.description:
Problem
Surfaced while reverse-engineering the stack for the cookbook PRD (cookbook PR #3082, prd/README.md § Known gaps).
extensions.py defines the shared cache as a _NullCache:
class _NullCache:
    """No-op cache used until a real cache backend (Valkey/Redis) is wired up."""
...
cache = _NullCache()  # extensions.py:20Every cache.get() / cache.set() call in the codebase silently does nothing. Docstrings around model-list fetching (e.g. services/model_service.py:125, blueprints/api_bp.py:57 — "Fetches fresh models from Gemini API and updates the cache") read as if caching is live, but nothing is ever cached. Valkey exists in the deployment, but only the Express layer uses it (rate limiting).
Impact
Model-list (and any other "cached") lookups hit the Gemini API on every request — extra latency and quota burn.
Docstrings and code structure imply behavior that doesn't exist, which misleads readers (it misled the PRD pass until the stub was found).
Options
Wire the cache to the existing Valkey instance (it's already provisioned for Express rate limiting).
Or keep the stub but make the intent explicit at call sites, and strip docstring claims of caching until it's real.
Filed by Claude on Adam's behalf

issues.summary: PUT /api/recipes/<id> cannot publish/unpublish — slug/is_public columns only written via POST upsert
issues.description:
Problem
Surfaced while reverse-engineering the stack for the cookbook PRD (cookbook PR #3082, prd/README.md § Known gaps).
The publish columns are written only on the POST/upsert path (repositories/db_recipe_repository.py:133-134 and :145-146). The PUT path deliberately skips the is_public column — the comment at db_recipe_repository.py:187-188 explains it guards against a guest smuggling is_public: true in the blob:
# PUT does not write the is_public column, but the blob must not
# carry a guest-smuggled is_public=true either.The guest-gating itself is correct (and _gate_is_public at line 30 handles it). But the asymmetry means an authenticated API client using PUT /api/recipes/<id> can never publish or unpublish a recipe, and cannot update its slug. Publishing works in production only because the SPA saves via the POST upsert.
Impact
The REST surface is inconsistent: PUT accepts a full recipe body, silently drops slug/is_public column changes, and returns 200.
Any future client (mobile app, script, MCP tool) that reasonably uses PUT for updates will "unpublish nothing and publish nothing" with no error.
Options
Make PUT write slug/is_public for authenticated owners (still routed through _gate_is_public).
Or declare PUT publish-neutral by design: strip slug/is_public from the accepted PUT body schema, document it in API.md, and point clients at a dedicated publish endpoint or the upsert.
Filed by Claude on Adam's behalf

issues.summary: Legacy POST /api/migrate is unauthenticated and reachable through the public proxy
issues.description:
Problem
Surfaced while reverse-engineering the stack for the cookbook PRD (cookbook PR #3082, prd/README.md § Known gaps).
blueprints/api_bp.py:189:
@api_bp.route("/migrate", methods=["POST"])
def run_migration():
    """Migrate all recipes in the recipes directory to the latest schema."""
    results = MigrationService.migrate_all_recipes()The route has no auth decorator — unlike the token-gated /api/admin/_ routes — and because Express proxies all of /api/_ verbatim, anyone on the internet can POST /api/migrate on production and trigger a schema migration pass over all stored recipes.
Impact
Unauthenticated write-path endpoint: a stranger can invoke bulk mutation of recipe data at will.
Even if migrate_all_recipes() is idempotent today, it's a free denial-of-service lever (bulk I/O over the whole recipe store per request) and a footgun if the migration logic ever becomes non-idempotent.
Options
Gate it with the same admin-token check as /api/admin/* (probably just move it under that blueprint).
Or delete the route entirely — schema migration is handled by Alembic/flask db upgrade via the flask-backend-migrate Cloud Run Job now, so a legacy HTTP trigger may have no remaining purpose.
Filed by Claude on Adam's behalf

issues.summary: /api/status DB probe uses raw-string SELECT 1 — raises under SQLAlchemy 2.x, so it always reports database: error
issues.description:
Problem
Surfaced while reverse-engineering the stack for the cookbook PRD (cookbook PR #3082, prd/README.md § Known gaps).
blueprints/api_bp.py:173 (inside api_status()):
db.session.execute("SELECT 1")SQLAlchemy 2.x removed implicit string-to-SQL coercion — Session.execute() with a plain string raises ArgumentError: Textual SQL expression 'SELECT 1' should be explicitly declared as text('SELECT 1'). The surrounding except Exception swallows it, so the probe reports database: {"status": "error"} even when the database is perfectly healthy.
Impact
The health endpoint lies: database is (almost certainly) always error in production, making it useless for monitoring and masking real outages behind a permanently-red signal.
Fix
from sqlalchemy import text
db.session.execute(text("SELECT 1"))One-line change plus a test asserting database.status == "connected" against the test DB — that test would have caught this.
Filed by Claude on Adam's behalf
